# UC-22.37.2 · Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-processing evidence

## Description

Cross-checks consent-withdrawal events against live processing telemetry. If a system continues to process a subject's data more than 24 hours after withdrawal, the UC raises a finding and opens a remediation task.

## Value

Withdrawals that do not propagate are a primary source of supervisory-authority enforcement; this UC turns a weak process control into a tested technical control.

## Implementation

(1) Instrument each consent-governed processing activity to emit subject_token and lawful_basis; (2) ingest withdrawals from the CMP; (3) run every 30 min; (4) on hit, open a SOAR case with the system owner. Indexes required: `index=app_activity`, `index=cmp`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: See subcategory preamble..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Instrument each consent-governed processing activity to emit subject_token and lawful_basis; (2) ingest withdrawals from the CMP; (3) run every 30 min; (4) on hit, open a SOAR case with the system owner.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=cmp action=withdraw earliest=-7d
| join subject_token [search index=app_activity (lawful_basis=consent) earliest=-7d]
| where _time>=withdrawal_time + 86400
| stats values(system) as systems_still_processing count by subject_token withdrawal_time
| where count>0
| table withdrawal_time subject_token systems_still_processing count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-processing evidence** — Withdrawals that do not propagate are a primary source of supervisory-authority enforcement; this UC turns a weak process control into a tested technical control.

Documented **Data sources**: See subcategory preamble. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: cmp.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=cmp, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• Filters the current rows with `where _time>=withdrawal_time + 86400` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` aggregates events by **subject_token withdrawal_time**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Filters the current rows with `where count>0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Output fields: count, subject_token, systems_still_processing, withdrawal_time.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare results with the audit register and the GRC system so evidence lines up with control owners, attestations, and retention policy.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Matrix of system × withdrawal-breach count (7d), single-value 'withdrawals propagated within 24h'.

## SPL

```spl
index=cmp action=withdraw earliest=-7d
| join subject_token [search index=app_activity (lawful_basis=consent) earliest=-7d]
| where _time>=withdrawal_time + 86400
| stats values(system) as systems_still_processing count by subject_token withdrawal_time
| where count>0
| table withdrawal_time subject_token systems_still_processing count
```

## Visualization

Matrix of system × withdrawal-breach count (7d), single-value 'withdrawals propagated within 24h'.
